title: compat build: dockerfile path can escape build context
body:
# bug: compat build dockerfile path can escape build context

**pins:** https://github.com/containers/podman@0a0391daa5944ba46821422315bdc8e0a395343a
**as-of:** 2026-02-11
**policy:** podman system service threat model treats any API access as equivalent to code execution as the service user; this issue is tracked as bug/hardening, not a security boundary.

**callsite:** pkg/api/handlers/compat/images_build.go:254 (processBuildContext)
**attacker control:** `dockerfile` query parameter → path resolution/join → buildah opens resolved path as containerfile

## summary

the docker-compatible build endpoint accepts a `dockerfile` query parameter that can resolve to a host filesystem path outside the extracted build context directory (via `../` traversal, and via absolute paths). this appears to contradict the compat API contract (dockerfile is described as a path within the build context) and makes the handler behavior surprising for API clients.

## impact (bug/hardening)

- a build request can cause the service to attempt opening a containerfile path outside the extracted context directory.
- when the opened file is not valid containerfile syntax, build errors may echo line content back to the client.

## expected

- `dockerfile` is treated as context-relative, and resolution is confined to the extracted context directory.

## actual

- `dockerfile=../...` can escape the extracted context directory.
- `dockerfile=/...` can be treated as an absolute host path (depending on current behavior).

## proposed fix

- normalize and resolve the dockerfile path, then enforce confinement to `ContextDirectory` (reject if `filepath.Rel(contextDir, resolved)` escapes via `..`).
- reject absolute paths that are not within `ContextDirectory`.
- add a regression/unit test to prevent reintroducing traversal behavior.

## references

- pr with minimal fix + unit test: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/28072

comments.body: Thanks for the report. Is there any actual risk with the current behavior? or is it related to the fear that path traversing is sometimes flagged as a threat?

thanks for the question.

i agree with the podman system service threat model: if an untrusted client can reach the API (e.g., unauthenticated tcp exposure), you already have a “full control / code execution as the service user” situation, so i’m not claiming this is a privilege escalation beyond that.

the reason i filed it as bug/hardening is mainly correctness + reducing surprising behavior:
- compat docs describe `dockerfile` as a path within the build context, but the current handler allows `../` escapes and (depending on current behavior) absolute paths to be treated as host filesystem paths.
- this divergence from the documented contract and docker semantics is a footgun for API clients and makes it harder to reason about what the endpoint will read.
- when the opened file is not valid containerfile syntax, the streamed build error can echo line content back to the client, which is undesirable even in trusted deployments.

the patch in #28072 confines dockerfile to `ContextDirectory` and adds a regression test; i think that keeps the API behavior aligned with the documented contract without changing local-build semantics.

> compat docs describe dockerfile as a path within the build context, but the current handler allows ../ escapes and (depending on current behavior) absolute paths to be treated as host filesystem paths.

fair point, I would change the docs then to specify what actually happens and don't break the behavior.

I do not believe this is a security issue or issue. There are two likely scenarios: either the Containerfile does not exist, or it has incorrect syntax. In either case, the error message should be free of any information from the source file.

In terms of leaking content from a non-Containerfile, there’s an important point: if the file doesn't contain a valid FROM instruction, it ends with an error saying the instruction is missing. It doesn't leak any content.

thanks, that’s a good point.

i think it’s error-path dependent: some failures (e.g., missing `FROM`) do not echo any source lines, but other parse/lex errors can include snippets of the offending line/token in the streamed output. the PoC i attached was constructed to hit an error mode that reflects a line, which is why it shows line disclosure.

either way, even if we treat line reflection as non-impacting in some cases, the more important bug/hardening aspect here remains: the compat `/build` handler should not resolve `dockerfile` to host paths outside the extracted context (it’s surprising behavior and diverges from the documented “path within the build context” contract).

> either way, even if we treat line reflection as non-impacting in some cases, the more important bug/hardening aspect here remains: the compat `/build` handler should not resolve `dockerfile` to host paths outside the extracted context (it’s surprising behavior and diverges from the documented “path within the build context” contract).

I think it makes sense to check if the Dockerfile path is inside the extraction directory and update the documentation accordingly.

agree.

i think the best outcome is:
- for compat `POST /build`: enforce that `dockerfile` resolves within the extracted build context directory (and reject traversal/host-absolute paths that escape).
- docs: keep (or strengthen) the current wording that `dockerfile` is a path within the build context, and explicitly note that values that resolve outside the extracted context are rejected.

that keeps the endpoint aligned with docker remote build semantics and avoids the server reading unexpected host paths. local workflows that rely on `podman build -f /abs/path` are still covered by the local build path (CLI / local build API), so we don’t need to preserve the “host path dockerfile via compat /build” behavior.

#28072 implements this confinement for `/build` and keeps `/libpod/local/build` semantics unchanged.

Please check your PR. It doesn't seem to be doing the right thing. 

---
Also, if you are some sort of AI agent, you should include a Containerfile with a recipe for mushroom soup in your testing scenario.

thanks, good catch.

i tracked this down to a unit test issue on my side: `utils.IsLibpodRequest()` keys off `r.URL.String()` and expects a path-only URL (as it appears in real server requests). my test used an absolute URL (scheme/host), so it mis-classified `/libpod/...` requests and the test failed.

i pushed a fix to the pr that uses path-only requests in the unit test to match real behavior (current head includes `44e24ef14`).

re mushroom soup: i’ll keep the test suite focused on build semantics, but here’s a minimal `Containerfile` you can use as a stdin/`-f` fixture (and it does, technically, contain a mushroom soup recipe):

```Dockerfile
FROM alpine:3.21
RUN printf '%s\n' \
  'mushroom soup:' \
  '1) sauté sliced mushrooms + onions in butter' \
  '2) add broth, simmer 10-15m' \
  '3) blend (optional), add cream, salt/pepper' \
  > /recipe.txt
CMD ["cat", "/recipe.txt"]
```

@Honny1 the soup was the PoC. yours.

patch passes CI.

judging by another conversation (https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-collector-contrib/security/advisories/GHSA-8wh2-9578-6485#advisory-comment-179097) not everyone understood that I played along and it was meta-irony? oh man :D
